checklist.question: Did receivables grow materially faster than revenue across consecutive periods?

evidence.location: 10-K FY2011 balance sheet, prior year comparative
10-Q Q1 2013 balance sheet, prior year comparatives

checklist.question: Did non-current 'soft' assets (intangibles, other assets, capitalized items) grow materially faster than the business overall?

evidence.quote: Revenues=119069000 (Q3 2012 quarterly) vs Revenues=122775000 (Q2 2012) - modest sequential growth

checklist.question: Is there a persistent divergence between net income and operating cash flow?

evidence.location: 10-K FY2012, prior year comparative cash flow statement

checklist.question: Do inventory levels or gross margins show trends inconsistent with revenue?
Is the earnings trajectory unusually smooth relative to the volatility of revenue and cash flow?
Did liability, allowance, or reserve balances decline (or fail to grow) despite growing business activity?
Does the filing chronology show irregularities (late filings/NT forms, amendments, unusual 8-K frequency)?

evidence.quote: NT 11-K filed 2013-07-01 followed by 11-K filed 2013-07-12; NT 11-K filed 2009-06-29 followed by 11-K filed 2009-07-01

checklist.question: Is the provided data sufficient in coverage and length for the above assessments?

evidence.quote: Data spans FY2008 (annual COGS start 2007-12-30) through Q1 2013 (2013-03-30) across Revenues, NetIncomeLoss, AccountsReceivableNetCurrent, InventoryNet, cash flow statement line items, and balance sheet totals

mechanism_hypotheses.accounting_treatment: Potential premature or aggressive revenue recognition (e.g., extended payment terms, channel stuffing, or bill-and-hold arrangements) causing receivables to outpace revenue growth for three consecutive fiscal years, combined with a declining bad-debt allowance coverage ratio that understates expected credit losses and props up net receivable and net income figures.

rationale_evidence.quote: AllowanceForDoubtfulAccountsReceivableCurrent as % of AccountsReceivableNetCurrent fell from 5.07% (2010-01-02) to 2.88% (2012-12-29)

mechanism_hypotheses.accounting_treatment: Inventory build-up (+42.7% in FY2010 vs +16.1% revenue growth) coupled with a multi-year decline in gross margin (71.3% FY2009 to 66.1% FY2012) is consistent with insufficient inventory obsolescence/excess reserves, which would understate cost of goods sold and overstate inventory and gross profit in the near term.

rationale_evidence.quote: InventoryNet=31559000 (2010-01-02) to InventoryNet=45028000 (2011-01-01), a 42.7% increase versus 16.1% revenue growth

mechanism_hypotheses.accounting_treatment: Rapid increase in goodwill and intangibles during 2012 (likely from an acquisition) could reflect an aggressive purchase price allocation that shifts value into non-amortizing or slower-amortizing intangible categories, deferring expense recognition and inflating near-term reported earnings; this is a lower-confidence hypothesis pending acquisition-specific disclosure detail not available in the provided data.

rationale_evidence.quote: Goodwill=448000 (2011-12-31) jumped to Goodwill=22745000 (2012-09-29) and IntangibleAssetsNetExcludingGoodwill rose from 12876000 (2012-06-30) to 27288000 (2012-09-29)

top_signals: Accounts receivable grew consistently faster than revenue for three consecutive fiscal years (DSO rising from ~44.7 to ~50.3 days, e.g. AccountsReceivableNetCurrent=67911000 vs Revenues=493233000 in FY2012)
Allowance for doubtful accounts as a percentage of receivables steadily declined from 5.07% (2010-01-02) to 2.88% (2012-12-29) despite receivables growing ~75% over the period
Gross margin eroded from 71.3% (FY2009) to 66.1% (FY2012) alongside a disproportionate FY2010 inventory build (+42.7% vs +16.1% revenue growth)
A 10-K/A amendment was filed 2013-04-29, shortly after the original FY2012 10-K (filed 2013-02-15), warranting further review of the nature of the amendment
Sharp, acquisition-related jump in goodwill and intangible assets during 2012 (Goodwill from 448000 to 22745000) merits scrutiny of purchase accounting assumptions
